title: Space-Based Solar Power: Beaming Energy From Orbit

slides.heading: Space-Based Solar Power
slides.body: Beaming clean energy from orbit to Earth — humanity's boldest energy idea is finally becoming real

slides.heading: The Big Idea
slides.body: Giant solar arrays in geostationary orbit collect sunlight 24/7 — no clouds, no night. The energy is converted to microwaves or laser beams and transmitted to receiving stations on Earth, where it becomes electricity.

slides.heading: Why Space? Sunlight Never Stops
slides.body: Satellites in geostationary orbit experience sunlight 99% of the year compared to 20–30% efficiency on the ground. Space solar could deliver up to 8× more energy per panel than the best Earth-based installations.

slides.heading: How the Transmission Works
slides.body: Solar energy is converted into microwave beams (around 2.45 GHz) that pass cleanly through clouds and atmosphere. Ground-based 'rectennas' — rectifying antennas — convert the microwaves back to electricity at over 85% efficiency.

slides.heading: Who Is Building It?
slides.body: The European Space Agency launched its SOLARIS initiative in 2023. China unveiled a roadmap targeting a 1 GW orbital plant by 2050. The UK's Space Energy Initiative and NASA are both funding feasibility studies and prototype hardware.

slides.heading: The 2023 Caltech Breakthrough
slides.body: Caltech's SSPP project became the first to successfully demonstrate wireless power transmission in space, beaming detectable energy to Earth from a small prototype satellite. It proved the core physics works outside a lab.

slides.heading: The Engineering Challenge
slides.body: A commercial plant would need square kilometers of ultra-thin solar panels assembled in orbit — far beyond any current launch capacity. Modular robotics, in-space manufacturing, and reusable heavy-lift rockets are all prerequisites.

slides.heading: Is the Beam Safe?
slides.body: Microwave beams would carry about 230 W/m² at the rectenna — less than standing in direct sunlight. The beam automatically defocuses if it drifts off-target. Birds and aircraft passing through would experience negligible heating.

slides.heading: The Cost Equation
slides.body: Early estimates put first-generation space solar at $2–4 per kWh — far too expensive today. But falling launch costs (SpaceX, reusability) and in-space manufacturing could bring this toward grid parity by the 2040s.

slides.heading: A Sun That Never Sets
slides.body: Space-based solar power could provide unlimited, weather-independent clean energy anywhere on Earth. The physics is proven — now it's an engineering and economics race. The next decade of launches will decide its future.
